project.description: NestJS enterprise-grade starter repository serving as the foundation for building a NestJS Learning Dashboard.

layers.name: API & Presentation Layer
layers.description: REST controllers and Data Transfer Objects (DTOs) that define the public interface of the application.

layers.name: Domain & Business Logic Layer
layers.description: Services and helpers that implement core business rules and orchestrate domain logic.

layers.name: Data Access & Persistence Layer
layers.description: Repositories, Prisma services, and database schemas for interacting with the persistent storage.

layers.name: Application Orchestration Layer
layers.description: Modules and root application files that wire together dependencies and bootstrap the framework.

layers.name: Infrastructure & Caching Layer
layers.description: Redis integration and global configuration for infrastructure services.

layers.name: Common & Cross-cutting Concerns
layers.description: Global filters, guards, interceptors, and utility functions shared across the application.

tour.title: 1. The Application Gateway
tour.description: Every enterprise app needs a secure and controlled entry point. In src/main.ts, we bootstrap the NestJS application and register global 'shields': ValidationPipes for input sanitization (Part 1.3) and ExceptionFilters for centralized error logging (Part 6.2). This ensures that no invalid data enters our system and no raw error traces leak to the client.

tour.title: 2. The Brain (Root Module)
tour.description: src/app.module.ts is the orchestrator. It connects feature modules (Auth, User, Catalog) with infrastructure modules (Prisma, Redis). This modular approach (Part 1.1) is critical for large teams, allowing features to be developed and scaled independently. Notice the CacheModule configuration—this is where we set up our centralized Redis cache (Part 4.2).

tour.title: 3. Data Integrity & ORM
tour.description: Data is the heart of an enterprise system. We use Prisma as our ORM (Part 2). The schema.prisma file defines our data models and relations with strict typing. This provides a single source of truth and enables managed migrations, ensuring our database state is always predictable and safe (Part 2.3).

tour.title: 4. Enterprise Authentication
tour.description: Security is not optional. Following Part 3.1 of the roadmap, our Auth service implements the Access + Refresh Token pattern. Access tokens are short-lived for security, while refresh tokens are stored (hashed) in the database to allow revoking sessions if needed. This is a standard practice for high-security applications.

tour.title: 5. Performance at Scale (Redis)
tour.description: As explained in Part 4.2, we don't use in-memory cache because it doesn't survive restarts or scale horizontally. src/redis/redis.service.ts provides a centralized caching layer. This allows all server instances to share the same cache, ensuring consistency across your entire cluster.

tour.title: 6. Business Logic & Service Pattern
tour.description: This is where the 'real work' happens. Following Part 1.1, business logic is strictly isolated in Services. Controllers only handle requests and responses, while Services interact with the Database (via Prisma) and the Cache (via Redis). This separation makes the code highly testable and maintainable.

tour.title: 7. Resilience & Observability
tour.description: Things will go wrong in production. The AllExceptionsFilter (Part 6.2) catches every error, logs it with structured data (useful for ELK/Datadog), and returns a consistent, sanitized response to the user. This is key to building a 'boring' and reliable system.

tour.title: 8. Access Control (RBAC)
tour.description: Finally, we enforce permissions using RolesGuards (Part 3.3). This stop shows how we use NestJS Reflectors to check for required roles on specific routes, ensuring that only authorized users (like Admins) can perform sensitive operations.
